YUFENG GUO: The General
Purpose Transformer, or GPT,

has really transformed
the AI landscape
over the last few years.

In this workshop, we'll
learn about its origins

and internal structure, and
we'll build one from scratch.

Hi, my name is Yufeng, and I'm
a developer advocate at Google.

Today, we'll check
out the process

of building a transformer
on JAX using the Flax NNX

library to build the model
architecture, Optax for loss

function and optimizer
creation, and train

on accelerated hardware with
the help of Orbax and XLA.

You'll get a taste of what
development on JAX looks like,

and be prepared to
take your next step

into the world of building
and training AI models.

Now, before we
dive into the code,

I want to set the
stage a little bit

and give some background
on transformers.

If you're already
familiar with this

and you want to skip
ahead to the coding,
please, by all means, feel free.
I certainly won't be offended.

When transformers and large
language models were first

coming onto the scene,
they were made specifically
for processing text.

Nowadays, many large
language models

are what we call
multi-modal, meaning

they can work with other modes
of communication, including
pictures, video, and audio.

But how does a computer really
know what any words mean?

It all comes down
to embeddings, a way

to turn human-understandable
concepts into numbers

that the computer can
process and interpret.

Initially, the entire
vocabulary that a language model

is expected to see is placed
sort of in random positions

throughout what we might
consider a higher dimensional
space.

This is called the
embedding space.

Now, how many dimensions
are we talking about?

It might be hundreds or even
thousands of dimensions.

This allows the
embedding to capture

a lot of understanding and
nuance about each and every term
in its vocabulary.

Then through the
training process,

involving looking at all of the
text in its training data set,

these text embeddings
are updated to reflect

the meaning of the words
and their relationships
to one another.

For instance, the
word jump might

be close to other physical
activities and exercises,

and it might also, perhaps
along a different dimension,

be close to terms about car
repair and jumping a car.

The key takeaway here is that
when machine learning models

handle text these
days, they're typically

looking at it through the
lens of an embedding space

rather than simply
the characters
of a word that you and I see.

If you want to learn
more about embeddings,

there are some great
detailed explanations

at the Machine Learning Crash
Course at developers.google.com.

And if you want to access some
existing embeddings that already

have a large vocabulary trained
and ready for you to use,

you can use text embeddings
from Google Cloud.

It even has multilingual
embedding models.

Now let's turn our attention
to the real reason you're here,
transformers.

Depending on who you ask, it
all started back in 2017 with

the research paper famously
titled "Attention is All You
Need," from Google Brain.

The concepts presented
in this paper lie

at the very heart of large
language models today,

and it led to further
research of variations

on the original
ideas, many of which

are still being developed
and explored to this day.

You can read the
paper if you'd like.
It's quite well-written.

In this video, we'll
go over some highlights

so we can better understand
what we'll be doing in the code.

The transformer architecture
focuses on a novel operation

called attention, hence
the title of the paper,

coupling it with a number
of other operations

that were commonplace among
machine learning models
at the time.
Let's dig into this further.

The task that the
researchers were

able to figure
out with attention

was a way to numerically
calculate and describe

the relationship between
words in the context
that they were being used.

Moreover, it allowed
the words themselves

to be that guide as
to the relationships
amongst each other.

The inputs to attention
were three vectors

called query, key, and
value, typically referred
to as Q, K and V.

You can think of the query
vector as describing,

for each word in a sentence
or phrase, what other words it
cares about.
What is it looking for?

The key vector is
a way to represent

what a word wants to present
about itself in response
to those other queries.

And the value vector encodes
what it kind of offers
in the event of a good match.

Another way to
describe it is perhaps

with a handshake, along
with a gift exchange.

The query is someone
reaching out their hand,

and the key is the other
hand coming out to meet it.

And if they
successfully match up,

then the value is what
is then handed over
if a handshake is established.

There are a couple
of other things

we see here in the
attention formula

aside from Q, K, and V. First,
there is the softmax function.
What is softmax?

Softmax normalizes the
values coming out of Q.K,

ensuring that they all
sum to 1, and shows us
which values are the highest.

So if one value is minorly
larger than the others,

it might end up
with a value of 0.8,
while the rest sum up to 0.2.
So it all comes together as 1.

If the two biggest values
are both about the same,

it's like a tie for first,
they might each have, say,

a value of 0.4, while the rest
of the values sum up to 0.2.

Second, we see
that Q.K is divided

by the square root
of D. D here refers

to the embedding dimension
used in our text embedding.

So if we had an embedding
dimension of, say, 64,

we'll divide the entire
dot product by 8.
Why are we doing this?

The reason is to scale
down the value that
gets passed into softmax.

Even though softmax is going to
normalize our values regardless,

the way that it
computes this means

that the higher the absolute
value of all the terms are,

the more those resulting
decimals get pulled to extremes.

So instead of having values
that are small and have

kind of small differences, it
might select the largest one,

even if it's only
larger by a little bit,
and just make it way bigger.

In other words, it makes the
distribution sharper or peakier.

All of this comes
together to make

what is called scaled
dot-product attention.

The bottom diagram uses
this and scales it up.

So when we talk about
multi-headed attention,

it's referring to multiple
parallel implementations

of attention, all happening
independently from one another.

This allows the model to
attend to different aspects

of the relationships
in the data.
